sections.heading: What is the AndroidManifest.xml?
sections.content: The AndroidManifest.xml is a required configuration file that lives at the root of every Android project's main source set (src/main/). It acts as the blueprint of your app — telling the Android system everything it needs to know about your app before it runs any code. Every Activity, Service, Broadcast Receiver, and Content Provider must be declared here, along with permissions, hardware requirements, and app metadata.

sections.heading: What the Manifest Declares
items: The app's package name (unique identifier on the device and Play Store)
All Activities, Services, Broadcast Receivers, and Content Providers
Permissions the app needs (e.g., INTERNET, CAMERA, LOCATION)
The minimum and target Android API levels
Required hardware and software features (e.g., camera, GPS)
The app's launcher icon and label
Intent filters that define how components respond to implicit Intents
App-level metadata (e.g., API keys, Firebase config)

sections.heading: Basic Manifest Structure
sections.content: The manifest file uses XML syntax and always starts with the <manifest> root element containing the package attribute. Inside it, the <application> element wraps all component declarations and app-wide settings. At minimum, every Android app must have at least one Activity declared with a MAIN action and LAUNCHER category so the system knows which screen to open when the user taps the app icon.

rows.description: Root element; contains the package name and versioning attributes
Declares a permission the app needs (e.g., INTERNET, READ_CONTACTS)
Declares required hardware/software features (e.g., camera, Bluetooth)
Specifies minSdkVersion and targetSdkVersion (usually set in Gradle)
Container for all app components and app-wide attributes like icon and theme
Declares an Activity component and its intent filters
Declares a Service component for background processing
Declares a BroadcastReceiver that listens for system or app events
Declares a ContentProvider for sharing data between apps
Key-value pairs for component or app-level configuration metadata

sections.heading: Important <application> Attributes
items: android:label – The app name displayed to the user on the home screen and in settings
android:icon – Reference to the launcher icon drawable (e.g., @mipmap/ic_launcher)
android:roundIcon – Round version of the launcher icon for supported launchers
android:theme – The default theme applied to all activities (e.g., @style/Theme.AppCompat)
android:allowBackup – Whether the app's data is backed up to Google Drive (true/false)
android:supportsRtl – Whether the app supports right-to-left layouts for RTL languages
android:networkSecurityConfig – Points to an XML file defining network security rules
android:name – Points to a custom Application subclass if you have one

sections.heading: Declaring an Activity in the Manifest

rows.description: Fully qualified or relative class name of the Activity (e.g., .MainActivity)
Title shown in the action bar or task switcher for this Activity
Whether other apps can launch this Activity (true for launcher Activity)
Locks orientation: portrait, landscape, or unspecified (default)
Controls how the Activity is instantiated: standard, singleTop, singleTask, singleInstance
Enables the Up button by declaring this Activity's logical parent
Defines what Intents this Activity responds to (action, category, data)

sections.heading: Intent Filters
sections.content: An intent filter tells the Android system what types of Intents a component can respond to. For the launcher Activity, you must include the MAIN action and LAUNCHER category so Android knows this is the entry point of your app. Other components can declare filters to respond to implicit intents like sharing, viewing URLs, or opening specific file types.

rows.description: Marks this Activity as the entry point of the application
Places the app icon in the device's launcher/home screen
Activity can display data (e.g., open a URL in a browser)
Activity can send data to another app (e.g., share text or image)
Activity lets the user select a piece of data (e.g., contact, image)
Required for Activities that respond to implicit intents
Allows the Activity to be started from a web browser via a link
Specifies URI scheme (http, https, custom) for deep link handling

sections.heading: App Permissions in the Manifest
items: Normal permissions are granted automatically at install time (e.g., INTERNET, VIBRATE)
Dangerous permissions require explicit user approval at runtime (e.g., CAMERA, LOCATION)
Declare all permissions inside <manifest> but outside <application> using <uses-permission>
android.permission.INTERNET – Required for any network/HTTP calls
android.permission.ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION – GPS-level precise location access
android.permission.CAMERA – Access to device camera hardware
android.permission.READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE – Read files from shared storage (pre-API 33)
android.permission.POST_NOTIFICATIONS – Required to show notifications on Android 13+

rows.examples: Used between apps from same developer

sections.heading: Declaring Other App Components
items: <service android:name='.MyService'> – Declares a background Service component
Add android:foregroundServiceType for foreground services on Android 9+
<receiver android:name='.MyReceiver' android:exported='false'> – Declares a BroadcastReceiver
Receivers with intent filters for system events should set exported='true' carefully
<provider android:name='.MyProvider' android:authorities='com.example.provider'> – Declares a ContentProvider
Providers require android:exported and android:authorities attributes to function
From Android 12 (API 31)+, all components with intent filters must explicitly set android:exported

rows.reason: Must be accessible from the device launcher
Should not be started by external apps
Prevent other apps from binding or starting the service
System needs to deliver broadcasts to it
Only expose if sharing data with other apps

sections.heading: Common Manifest Mistakes to Avoid
items: Forgetting to declare a new Activity — causes ActivityNotFoundException crash at runtime
Missing android:exported on components with intent filters on API 31+ — causes build failure
Not declaring INTERNET permission — causes NetworkOnMainThreadException or silent failures
Declaring dangerous permissions only in manifest without runtime request — permission denied
Setting minSdkVersion too low without testing on older devices — compatibility crashes
Hardcoding API keys in meta-data without securing them — security vulnerability
Leaving debug-only permissions (e.g., READ_LOGS) in release builds

sections.heading: Merged Manifest: How Libraries Add to Your Manifest

rows.description: Android merges manifests from your app, libraries, and SDKs into one final manifest at build time
In Android Studio, open AndroidManifest.xml and click the 'Merged Manifest' tab at the bottom
Use tools:replace or tools:remove attributes to override library manifest entries
These libraries automatically inject their own permissions and components via manifest merger
Removes a specific element injected by a library from the final merged manifest

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: The AndroidManifest.xml is the foundation of every Android app. It registers all components, declares permissions, and defines how the system interacts with your app. A well-configured manifest prevents runtime crashes and ensures your app behaves correctly across different Android versions and devices. Always double-check the manifest after adding new components, libraries, or permissions.
